event: Expulsion of Heliodorus from the Temple
symbols: heavenly horseman and angels — divine intervention to protect the sacred treasury
trampled Heliodorus — worldly power defeated by heavenly force
temple columns and architecture — the sacred space God defends from sacrilege
scattered treasures — the temple funds Heliodorus attempted to seize
figures fleeing and falling — the chaos wrought when mortals defy divine will
light from above — God's active presence defending his holy place
description: A large dramatic Baroque composition depicting a chaotic scene inside a grand columned temple. In the center and foreground, figures are thrown to the ground in violent disarray. A heavenly figure on horseback, accompanied by flying angelic beings, descends from the upper left in a blaze of light, driving figures downward. Soldiers and civilians scatter and fall across the temple floor. The architecture features massive columns and ornate classical structures. The scene is filled with dynamic movement, swirling drapery, and strong contrasts of light and shadow.
theological_description: The Expulsion of Heliodorus from the Temple, recorded in 2 Maccabees 3:7-40, narrates the divine defense of the Jerusalem Temple treasury against the Seleucid official Heliodorus, sent by King Seleucus IV to confiscate the temple funds. When Heliodorus entered the treasury, 'there appeared to them a horse with a terrifying rider... and it rushed furiously at Heliodorus and struck at him with its front hooves' (2 Maccabees 3:25), while two young men of surpassing strength flogged him. This event demonstrates the theology of divine protection of sacred space — God actively defends his temple against sacrilege. The episode resonates with Jesus's cleansing of the Temple (John 2:13-17), where Christ himself drives out those who profane his Father's house. The theological principle is consistent throughout Scripture: God's holiness will not tolerate the desecration of what is consecrated to him. The psalmist declares 'The Lord is in his holy temple; the Lord's throne is in heaven' (Psalm 11:4), and those who violate sacred space encounter divine judgment. Heliodorus's expulsion prefigures the eschatological judgment when all that opposes God will be overthrown.

The measuring and protection of God's temple — the eschatological dimension of divine protection of sacred space
